perturbation.original_text: Warrants and undertake that it is solely responsible for ensuring that the Personal Data is processed by it in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”) from the date that it is received from the Disclosing Party; (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient shall ensure that, prior to using or processing the Personal Data in such manner, it will ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act;
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the warranty to ensure personal data is processed in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 and the obligation to comply with the Act if processing data for a different purpose, the clause becomes non-compliant. This omission removes the legally required protection of personal data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Data Protection Act 2018 (which replaced the Data Protection Act 1998) and the GDPR
perturbation.law_citation: Data Protection Act 2018, Schedule 1, Part 1, paragraph 1
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause acknowledges the Data Protection Act 1998. By removing the clause requiring compliance, the modified text fails to ensure lawful processing of personal data as mandated by the Data Protection Act 2018 (which superseded the 1998 Act) and the GDPR, specifically Schedule 1, Part 1, paragraph 1, which outlines basic principles for processing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Data protection: The UK's data protection legislation - GOV.UK Cookies on GOV.UK We use some essential cookies to make this website work. We’d like to set additional cookies to understand how you use GOV.UK, remember your settings and improve government services. We also use cookies set by other sites to help us deliver content from their services. You have accepted additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. You have rejected additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Data protection Skip contents The UK's data protection legislation Data protection legislation controls how your personal information is used by organisations, including businesses and government departments. In the UK, data protection is governed by the UK General Data Protection Regulation (UK GDPR) and the Data Protection Act 2018 . Everyone responsible for using personal data has to follow strict rules called ‘data protection principles’ unless an exemption applies. There is a guide to the data protection exemptions on the Information Commissioner’s Office ( ICO ) website . Anyone responsible for using personal data must make sure the information is: used fairly, lawfully and transparently used for specified, explicit purposes used in a way that is adequate, relevant and limited to only what is necessary accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date kept for no longer than is necessary handled in a way that ensures appropriate security, including protection against unlawful or unauthorised processing, access, loss, destruction or damage There is stronger legal protection for more sensitive information, such as: race ethnic background political opinions religious beliefs trade union membership genetics biometrics (where used for identification) health sex life or orientation There are separate safeguards for personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences. Your rights Under the legislation, you have rights in relation to your personal data, with some exceptions. These include the right to: be informed about how your data is being used access personal data have incorrect data updated have data erased stop or restrict the processing of your data data portability (allowing you to get and reuse your data for different services) object to how your data is processed in certain circumstances You also have rights when an organisation is using your personal data for: automated decision-making processes (without human involvement) profiling, for example to predict your behaviour or interests If you’re concerned about how an organisation is handling your personal data Contact the ICO for advice or to make a complaint. ICO Telephone: 0303 123 1113 Textphone: 18001 0303 123 1113 Monday to Friday, 9am to 5pm Find out about call charges Information Commissioner’s Office Wycliffe House Water La
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit obligation to comply with the Data Protection Act 1998 when processing personal data. The scraped snippet 2 states that data protection legislation controls how personal information is used and that everyone responsible for using personal data has to follow strict rules called ‘data protection principles’. By omitting the original clause, the contract fails to ensure lawful processing of personal data, which directly contradicts the principles outlined in the UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018, as described in the law explanation.

perturbation.original_text: It informs its Representatives of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information before disclosure; (b) It procures that its Representatives shall, in relation to any Confidential Information disclosed to them, comply with this Agreement as if they were SAMPLE4 the Recipient and, if the Disclosing Party so requests, procure that any relevant Representative enters into a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Party on terms equivalent to those contained in this Agreement ; and (c) It keeps a written record of these Representatives, and it shall at all times be liable for the failure of any Representative to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: It informs its Representatives of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information before disclosure; (b) It procures that its Representatives shall, in relation to any Confidential Information disclosed to them, comply with this Agreement as if they were SAMPLE4 the Recipient; and (c) It keeps a written record of these Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the Disclosing Party's ability to request a confidentiality agreement from the Representatives and removing that the Recipient shall at all times be liable for the failure of any representative to comply with the terms of this agreement, the clause is weakened. This increases legal risk for the disclosing party.
perturbation.law_citation: Contract (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, Section 1
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause allows the Disclosing Party to request a confidentiality agreement directly with the Recipient's Representatives. This omission removes that right and removes the liability of the Recipient. This can contradict Section 1 of the Contract (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, which allows third parties to enforce contract terms under certain conditions. By weakening the direct contractual link with the representatives, the Disclosing Party's ability to enforce confidentiality could be hindered. Removing the Representative's liability further exacerbates this issue.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language ensured that the Disclosing Party could request a confidentiality agreement directly with the Recipient's Representatives and held the Recipient liable for any failures by the Representatives to comply with the agreement. The changed text removes both of these provisions, weakening the Disclosing Party's protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation states that removing these provisions potentially contradicts Section 1 of the Contract (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, by hindering the Disclosing Party's ability to enforce confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall establish and maintain adequate security measures (including any reasonable security measures proposed by the Disclosing party from time to time) to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall establish and maintain security measures to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that the Recipient should include any reasonable security measures proposed by the Disclosing Party from time to time, the clause is weakened. This creates legal risk for the disclosing party because they no longer have any say in the security measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: GDPR Article 32 requires implementing appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including considering the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing. The original clause allowed the Disclosing Party to propose reasonable security measures, ensuring a collaborative approach to data security. Removing this element weakens the obligation on the Recipient to consider all relevant factors in determining appropriate security measures, potentially leading to a GDPR violation if the implemented measures are inadequate given the nature of the confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 32 GDPR – Security of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing. In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. Adherence to an approved code of conduct as referred to in Article 40 or an approved certification mechanism as referred to in Article 42 may be used as an element by which to demonstrate compliance with the requirements set out in paragraph 1 of this Article. The controller and processor shall take steps to ensure that any natural person acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data does not process them except on instructions from the controller, unless he or she is required to do so by Union or Member State law. Suitable Recitals ( 75 ) Risks to the Rights and Freedoms of Natural Person
perturbation.justification: The original text required the Recipient to consider security measures proposed by the Disclosing party, while the changed text removes this requirement. The Law Explanation states that this weakens the obligation on the Recipient to consider relevant factors, potentially leading to a GDPR violation if the implemented measures are inadequate. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (GDPR Article 32) mandates 'appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including considering the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing'. By removing the Disclosing party's input, the Recipient may not adequately consider all factors necessary for appropriate security measures, creating a legal contradiction because a legally mandated consumer protection is omitted.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non-contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with English law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation shall be governed by and construed in accordance with English law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing '(including non-contractual disputes or claims)', the scope of the governing law is narrowed. This can create ambiguity about whether non-contractual claims (e.g., tort claims) related to the agreement are also governed by English law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Rome II Regulation (Article 4(1)) dictates the law applicable to non-contractual obligations. The original clause explicitly included non-contractual disputes under English law. By omitting this phrase, the modified text creates uncertainty and potential conflict with the Rome II Regulation, which might lead to a different jurisdiction's law being applied to non-contractual claims, contrary to the parties' initial intentions. Although Brexit has occurred, the Rome II Regulation still holds persuasive authority and influence in interpreting applicable law, particularly for contracts predating Brexit and international disputes.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly includes 'non-contractual disputes or claims' under English law, providing clarity. By omitting this phrase, the modified text introduces ambiguity, potentially conflicting with the Rome II Regulation regarding the law applicable to non-contractual obligations, as explained in the Law Explanation. Although the scraped snippets are not helpful as Snippet 1 is empty and Snippet 2 is an error page, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest the omission creates a loophole regarding non-contractual claims, satisfying the definition of an Omission Legal Contradiction. This alteration weakens the intended scope of governing law, which could lead to the application of a different jurisdiction's law to non-contractual claims, thus undermining the original intention. Given the explanation, this falls under the Omission Legal Contradiction category.
